questions.question: Define runtime polymorphism and explain how it is achieved in C++.
keyPoints: Runtime polymorphism allows function call resolution at runtime
Achieved through virtual functions and base class pointers/references
Uses vtable and vptr for dynamic binding
Function to execute determined by actual object type at runtime

questions.question: What is the difference between function overloading and function overriding?
keyPoints: Overloading: same name, different parameters, compile-time
Overriding: same signature, different class, runtime via virtual
Overloading provides compile-time polymorphism
Overriding provides runtime polymorphism

questions.question: Explain the concept of pure virtual functions and abstract classes with an example.
keyPoints: Pure virtual function declared with =0
Makes class abstract (cannot be instantiated)
Serves as contract for derived classes
Derived classes must implement all pure virtual functions or become abstract
Example: Shape class with virtual area() = 0

questions.question: What is a virtual destructor? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Virtual destructor declared with virtual keyword before destructor
Ensures proper cleanup when object deleted through base pointer
Calls derived class destructor first, then base class destructor
Prevents memory leaks in inheritance hierarchies
Without virtual, only base destructor called (slicing/dangling resources)

questions.question: Explain the vtable and vptr mechanism in C++.
keyPoints: vtable: lookup table per class with virtual functions
Contains addresses of virtual function implementations
vptr: hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable
Set by constructor when object is created
Runtime function call follows: object→vptr→vtable→function address

questions.question: What is slicing problem? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Slicing occurs when derived object assigned to base object by value
Derived portion is lost, only base portion remains
Prevent by using pointers or references to base class
Use virtual functions with base class pointers to maintain polymorphism

questions.question: Differentiate between upcasting and downcasting with safety considerations.
keyPoints: Upcasting: derived to base - always safe, implicit conversion
Downcasting: base to derived - unsafe, requires explicit cast
Use dynamic_cast for safe downcasting (returns nullptr on failure
Downcasting should be avoided when possible; use virtual functions instead

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating runtime polymorphism using base class pointers and virtual functions. Create a base class 'Animal' with a virtual function 'sound()' and derived classes 'Dog' and 'Cat' overriding this function. Show how calling through base pointer invokes the appropriate derived class function.
keyPoints: Define base class Animal with virtual function sound()
Create derived classes Dog and Cat with their own sound() implementations
Create base class pointer pointing to derived objects
Call sound() through base pointer - demonstrates polymorphism
Show output confirming derived functions are called
Use virtual destructor in base class

questions.question: Explain the concept of abstract classes in C++. Create an abstract class 'Shape' with pure virtual function 'area()' and derive classes 'Rectangle' and 'Circle' from it. Demonstrate that abstract class cannot be instantiated while derived classes can.
keyPoints: Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function (=0)
Pure virtual function has no implementation in abstract class
Cannot create objects of abstract class
Derived classes must implement all pure virtual functions
Demonstrate with working code: Shape* ptr; Rectangle r; Circle c; ptr = &r; ptr->area();

questions.question: What will be the output of the following code? Explain the concept of dynamic dispatch. class Base { public: virtual void show() { cout << "Base"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() { cout << "Derived"; } }; int main() { Base* b = new Derived(); b->show(); delete b; }
keyPoints: Output will be 'Derived' due to runtime polymorphism
Virtual function enables late binding
Base pointer points to Derived object
At runtime, vptr points to Derived's vtable
show() resolved to Derived::show() at runtime
Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for a banking system with Account as abstract base class and SavingsAccount and CurrentAccount as derived classes. Include pure virtual function calculateInterest(). Demonstrate polymorphism by processing different account types through base class pointer array.
keyPoints: Abstract class Account with data members and pure virtual calculateInterest()
SavingsAccount and CurrentAccount derive and implement calculateInterest()
Create array of Account pointers pointing to different account objects
Loop through array calling calculateInterest() - demonstrates polymorphism
Virtual destructor in Account class for proper cleanup
